pathophysiology_summary: Head lice infestation is an infestation of the scalp by Pediculus humanus capitis; the intense itch is a hypersensitivity reaction to louse saliva, typically worst at the occiput and behind the ears. Diagnosis requires seeing LIVE lice; adherent nits (eggs) firmly attached to hair shafts near the scalp support it (unlike dandruff, which brushes off). It is benign and non-dangerous (no systemic illness). The ground-truth tasks are (1) confirming live lice and distinguishing from seborrhoeic dermatitis (flaky, non-adherent) and tinea capitis (scaly patches, black-dot hair loss, kerion), (2) correct management — a topical pediculicide (permethrin/malathion or dimeticone) with a repeat treatment in 7 days to kill newly hatched lice, systematic wet-combing with conditioner, treating affected household contacts and decontaminating bedding, and (3) screening secondary bacterial infection from scratching (impetiginisation/cellulitis). Paediatric antihistamine dosing (for sleep/itch) is weight-based and prescribed in person.

differential.evidence_basis: Visible live lice and firmly adherent nits with occipital/retroauricular itch.
Flakes brush off and there are no live insects — argues against.
Scaly patches with hair loss/kerion — not seen here.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Head lice leads (live lice seen); secondary infection must be screened.

differential.evidence_basis: Live lice/adherent nits, no honey-crusting/pus/fever — uncomplicated head lice; scratch marks only.
No crusting/pus/cellulitis; escalation target if it develops.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative secondary-infection screen, uncomplicated head lice leads; pediculicide + wet-combing + repeat + household measures.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; benign; needs a repeat treatment at day 7 and household measures.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign head lice.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection — honey-coloured crusting, pustules, hot spreading redness (cellulitis), fever, or markedly swollen tender neck nodes
red_flags.clinical_significance: Excoriated scalp can become impetiginised/cellulitic and need antibiotics.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Diagnosing on nits alone / anti-dandruff shampoo
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Nits without live lice may be old; anti-dandruff shampoo does not treat lice — confirm live lice and use a pediculicide.
Omitting the day-7 repeat treatment lets newly hatched lice re-establish the infestation.
Sedating-antihistamine dosing for itch/sleep is weight-based and prescribed in person.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Pediculicide + wet-combing + repeat at day 7 + household measures; GP/ED if secondary bacterial infection develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing secondary bacterial infection; treating without a repeat course.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Confirm live lice. Treat with a topical pediculicide (permethrin/malathion or dimeticone) and REPEAT after 7 days; combine with systematic wet-combing with conditioner. Treat affected household contacts; decontaminate bedding/clothing. Manage secondary infection if present.
